clean_story:
Electric potential. Energy in waiting.

The word volt—named after Alessandro Volta—measures how hard electricity pushes through a wire, like water pressure in a hose. A 9-volt battery isn’t "stronger" than a AA; it just packs its push into smaller bursts.

Here’s the unseen labor: Voltage doesn’t flow—it enables flow. A dead car battery reads 12 volts but delivers no current; a live one uses those same volts to spin the starter motor. High-voltage power lines (765,000 volts) lose less energy over distance, like shouting across a canyon instead of whispering. Meanwhile, your phone’s 5-volt charger tricks lithium ions into marching in order, storing energy without explosions.

Precision matters. A neuron fires at ~70 millivolts; a lightning bolt hits 300 million. Both rely on the same rule: Voltage is the difference that forces movement.
Three related subjects are current vs voltage, battery chemistry secrets, and how grids balance power.
